column1: # GENERATED by `python -m puckworks.paper3.evidence_graph --write` from EVIDENCE_LINKS.json + the live registry. DO NOT EDIT BY HAND — edits are overwritten and fail CI. Curate EVIDENCE_LINKS.json instead.

column18: observable
column19: claim
column20: claim_not_supported

column18: Minimum composite porosity (eps_min vs eps0) and the 9-bar Q(t) RMSE vs the measured Waszkiewicz trace (g/s).
column19: Adding the parameter-free mo2023_2 swelling branch behaves as the framework predicts: the composite porosity closes below eps0 and the 9-bar Q(t) residual jumps from ~0.12 to ~0.65 g/s (worse than the flat null ~0.603), diagnosing that mo2023_2's fixed-dP fresh-grain swelling is mis-scaled for an already-swollen saturated pre-wet rig.
column20: Does NOT show swelling improves or explains the 9-bar flow — the opposite: passing certifies the swelling branch makes the prediction worse and must not be tuned to hide the residual.

column18: Mass flow Q(t) at 9 bar vs the measured Waszkiewicz trace over the 15-95 s saturated window (RMSE, g/s).
column19: In that extraction-only reduction the coupled model's predicted 9-bar Q(t) reproduces the measured Waszkiewicz 9-bar flow ramp with RMSE ~0.113 g/s (gate <0.13), matching the poroelastic component's own reconstruction (rung 4).
column20: A passing RMSE is NOT independent validation of the kappa(t) mechanism; it is the same-rig reconstruction the poroelastic component already achieved, inherited unchanged.

column18: Structural identity of the coupled model's extraction-only Phi(t)/Q(t) closure to the standalone poroelastic component (a code property).
column19: With the swelling/compaction/fines-loss branches disabled, the coupled shared-porosity synthesis reduces exactly to its degenerate special case waszkiewicz2025.poroelastic (extraction-only Phi(t)=m_d(t)/m0 through the same poroelastic closure), adding no new behaviour in this limit.
column20: Does NOT validate the full four-branch synthesis nor show the poroelastic physics is independently correct — only that the two models are indistinguishable when losses are off.

column18: Darcy permeability k of a plane-Poiseuille channel in lattice units vs the closed-form analytic value h^2/12.
column19: The D3Q19 TRT lattice-Boltzmann permeability kernel reproduces the exact plane-Poiseuille channel permeability h^2/12 to within 0.5% (reported +0.03%).
column20: Validates solver numerics against an analytic channel only — not against coffee/permeability data or sphere-drag at production resolution.

column18: Generated solid fraction vs target, grain radius in voxels, and heterogeneity-field mean/std.
column19: The overlapping-sphere pack generator hits its target solid fraction (0.55 -> 0.551), produces an admissible boolean pack at resolution (grain radius >= 10 voxels), and its columnar heterogeneity field is zero-mean / unit-std.
column20: It does not validate the pack against measured coffee microstructure (CT/tomography) or permeability; it is a generator self-consistency / admissibility check.

column18: Held-out relative EY deviation (measured yield below the homogeneous-flow model) at the held-out grind.
column19: The sigma(GS) fines closure, fit on two of Cameron's three Fig-5 grinds, predicts the held-out grind's relative EY deviation to <2 pp (leave-one-out over GS 1.1/1.3/1.5; observed max held-out error ~1.1 pp).
column20: It does not externally validate the streamtube model nor extrapolate beyond dial 1.1-1.5; the held-out fold is 1 of only 3 same-campaign points.

column18: Extraction yield EY (%) via two internal accounting routes plus an inventory upper bound, all computed from the simulation and module constants.
column19: The cameron2020 BDF two-population solver conserves solute mass (cup-mass EY agrees with solid-depletion EY to within 0.5 pp) and the predicted EY stays strictly below the model's own per-bed soluble-inventory ceiling.
column20: Does NOT establish that the model's EY matches any measured espresso yield — it is a numerical mass-budget self-consistency check, not empirical validation.

column18: Location of the q_inf(p0) peak vs the steepest-slope knee of the beta(q) threshold, plus non-monotonicity of q_inf(p0).
column19: In Fasano's digitized figures the peak of the asymptotic-discharge curve q_inf(p0) (Fig 8.6) sits at the flow where the detachment threshold beta(q) (Fig 8.7) drops steeply, the structural link the paper's Corollary 8.2 requires.
column20: It does not reproduce the paper's model curve from the governing equations, identifies no parameter, and asserts nothing about physical reality.

column18: Discharge trace q(t), free-boundary position s(t), global fines mass balance, and q_inf vs injection pressure p0.
column19: A free-boundary fines-migration solver with puckworks' own (invented) closures exhibits Fasano's analytic structure: global mass balance <1%, q(t) monotone non-increasing, s(t)>= s_min, and a non-monotone q_inf(p0) with an interior peak.
column20: It does not reproduce Fasano's Fig 8.6 curve, does not fit or validate against any measured flow trace, and identifies no physical parameter.

column18: Peak discharge rate (mL/s) within each of the three experimental segments (direct / off-on resume / inverted).
column19: In the digitized Fig 8.4 reversible-chamber experiment the direct segment and the chamber-inverted segment both replay a large transient discharge peak while the pressure-off/on resume segment stays at the low plateau — the fines-counter-migration reversal signature.
column20: It does not independently reproduce the reversal from a mechanism, does not quantify amplitudes against controlled data, and provides no parameter identification.

column18: First-liquid / bed-saturation time (s): observed = time cup weight first exceeds 0.5 g; predicted = t_saturate when the modelled front reaches bed depth L, at porosity endpoints 0.173 and 0.322.
column19: A sharp-front infiltration prediction, driven by the DE1 shot's own measured pressure trace and a permeability from that shot's fitted kappa, yields a bed-saturation-time window (across an a-priori porosity bracket 0.173-0.322) that brackets the observed first-drip time (~7 s).
column20: Does NOT demonstrate a parameter-free prediction independent of the evaluated data: k and P(t) are from the same shot, so 'parameter-free' holds only in that no parameter was tuned to hit the first-drip time itself.

column18: Wetting-front depth s(t) and headspace height H(t) (mm) over the shot, vs the paper's fitted ODE curves and vs digitized CT measurements.
column19: The machine-mode solver's front s(t) and headspace H(t) match Foster's own fitted ODE curves to <0.2 mm RMSE and each bracket at least 4 digitized CT data points within their (>=0.5 mm) error bars.
column20: It does not establish parameter-free or out-of-sample prediction of the wetting-front trajectory; the paper validates model form, not parameter-free prediction.

column18: Normalized bed flow Q/Qm = min(Q_p, f(H,s))/Q_m vs experiment time.
column19: The re-implemented Foster machine-mode ODE (pump characteristic + trapped-air headspace) reproduces the paper's own Fig 15 normalized bed-flow curve, dipping to Q/Qm~0.181 at t~2.0 s and recovering, to RMSE <0.01 over the t_p..t_s window.
column20: It does not validate the mid-shot flow minimum against any measured flow trace, nor establish that pump+headspace dynamics cause real early-shot flow dips (CT validation is a separate gate).

column18: Two scalar times (s): ponding time t_p and saturation time t_s, model-computed vs the authors' reported values.
column19: The staged pump/headspace machine-mode ODE, run from the transcribed Table I/II parameters and offset by the fitted t_shift=0.796 s, reproduces the authors' reported ponding time t_p=0.823 s and saturation time t_s=6.669 s.
column20: Not a test against independent experimental first-drip/CT timing — it reproduces the paper's own reported model-output numbers from the paper's own fitted parameters.

column18: The algebraic form of the capacitance coefficient B, and kappa from Eq 6.14 at nominal card inputs.
column19: The reduced solver's capacitance coefficient B equals phi_l/phi_T + phi_f/phi_T with NO epsilon factor (the adjudicated no-eps form), and recomputing Eq 6.14 at 9.2 bar yields the adjudicated kappa ~2.2e-15 m^2 rather than the printed 2.2e-16 (decade-typo correction).
column20: It does not validate kappa against any independent permeability measurement nor the model against extraction data; it establishes only self-consistency of the adjudicated form.

column18: Per-vial solubles mass (g) for vials 3-15, total solubles mass, and existence of the saturated exit plateau.
column19: The reduced solve produces a post-first-drip saturated exit plateau and reconstructs the C1 per-vial solubles masses — total solute within 5% of the 14-shot mean and >=8 of 13 vials within 1 SD.
column20: It does not establish independent or out-of-sample predictive accuracy — the forthcoming time-resolved companion dataset is the pending independent gate.

column18: Sign of pathway EY divergence, qualitative shape of EY vs grind (peak+decline vs plateau) at two rho_c, and two nondimensional tau_shot values.
column19: The two-pathway extraction->porosity->permeability model reproduces lee2023's documented sign/shape behaviour — seed amplification, an interior EY(g) peak with fine-side decline ONLY at the imposed unphysical rho_c=798, a plateau at physical rho_c=399, and the reviewer's tau_shot cross-check values (1.12, 0.48).
column20: It does not validate the model against extraction data or reproduce the amplitude of the fine-grind EY dip; it is code/source-equation reproduction, not a data fit.

column18: E_oven (%) and equilibrium E (%) vs brew ratio R_brew (>=3) from Fig 4, plus the inequality K*E_max < cameron_inventory_ceiling.
column19: The Eq-22 oven-drying kernel reproduces the Fig 4 oven branch (MAPE<0.15), the equilibrium E is flat at ~21%, and the immersion equilibrium ceiling K*E_max (0.215) sits below cameron's per-bed soluble-inventory ceiling.
column20: The ceiling comparison does not establish either ceiling against data and is not a conflict resolution; nothing here validates espresso/flow extraction (Liang is immersion-only).

column18: The dimensionless equilibrium desorption product K*E_max from equilibrium TDS vs brew ratio R_brew for full-immersion brewing.
column19: Re-fitting the lumped equilibrium product K*E_max via Eq. 11 to the digitized Fig. 3 TDS-vs-brew-ratio data (R_brew>=3) recovers ~0.215, the card's reported value.
column20: Does NOT validate the desorption model against independent data or for espresso/flow — only that the re-estimate reproduces the paper's reported value from the same curve.

column18: Yield (%) vs beverage mass M_c (g), type-M, q=2/3/4 mL/s, M_c<30 g; within-bars count, shape spread, mass-balance floor.
column19: The depth-resolved coupled through-flow bed reproduces mo2023_2's type-M fixed-flow yield-vs-beverage-mass (Fig 8, M_c<30 g) within the digitized replicate error bars at 5/9 points and with tighter shape-spread than the reduced lumped bed, while staying mass-conserving.
column20: It does not validate the swelling mechanism itself, does not hold for M_c>30 g, and is not an out-of-sample test.

column18: Mean yield (%) over the M_c 18-32 g window, across q (2 vs 4 mL/s) and across powders E/M/F at matched q.
column19: The digitized mo2023_2 fixed-flow yield data exhibit the paper's stated qualitative trends: slower flow gives higher yield at matched beverage mass, and finer grind (E>M>F) gives higher yield, monotone with no fine-grind dip.
column20: It supports no model prediction and no quantitative accuracy; it merely confirms monotone trend directions already stated by the paper.

column18: Per-powder relative error between the recomputed CK k0 and the tabulated Table 2 k0 (m^2).
column19: The closed-form Carman-Kozeny k0 with eps_b=0.17, n=0.5 reproduces mo2023_2 Table 2 k0 from the Table 1 Sauter diameter for all four powders to <3%.
column20: It does not validate the swelling mechanism, does not compare k0 to any measured permeability, and says nothing about flow during a shot.

column18: Fixed-dp flow-decay ratio q(60s)/q(0) (dp/mu/L cancelled) per powder E/H/M/F, plus the scalar s_m.
column19: The re-implemented swelling-PDE -> Eq.21 bed-porosity -> Carman-Kozeny chain reproduces the paper's SIMULATED Fig 3(a) fixed-dp flow-decay ratio q(60s)/q(0) for all four powders within 20%, preserves the coarser-throttles-less ordering E<H<M<F, and matches the closed-form max swelling s_m=3.57%.
column20: It does not test the swelling-throttled-flow mechanism against any measured flow-decay data; the ordering is a reproduced property of the source's model, not an experimentally confirmed fact.

column18: End-of-shot yield (fraction of extractable inventory) with swelling on vs off at q=2/3/4 mL/s (fixed-q), contrasted with the fixed-dP Carman-Kozeny flow-decay ratio.
column19: At fixed flow the implemented mo2023_2 two-population model gives nearly identical yield with swelling on vs off (max relative difference <5%) because swelling raises grain porosity/D_p (~+15%) but grows R^2 (~+7%) and these offset — reproducing the paper's Fig. 2 model behaviour.
column20: Does NOT validate the swelling mechanism or absolute yields against experiment — only that the code exhibits the offsetting-timescale insensitivity the paper asserts at fixed flow.

column18: Predicted-vs-measured cylindrical Delta_p (Pa) at the two permeability anchors.
column19: moroney2015's Kozeny-Carman closure (k_h = k_sv1^2 phi_h^3 / (36 kappa (1-phi_h)^2)) with the measured kappa=3.1 and the Table-2 compacted phi_h reproduces the measured cylindrical Delta_p at Q=250 mL/min for both grinds (JK, Cimbali #20) within ~5%.
column20: Internal consistency of the intaken Tables 1/2 only (phi_h was flow-matched, so this is post-fit-same-data, not an independent permeability validation); a drip-filter chamber, NOT an espresso ratio -> reference strength; establishes no espresso claim.

column18: Solute mass-budget closure, exit-concentration shape/timescales, and corrected-vs-reported intragranular diffusivity relative to free aqueous diffusion.
column19: A method-of-lines solve of moroney2019's 1-D two-grain LDF cylindrical reduction (Eqs 17-27) with the cooper2021-corrected h_sl closes the solute mass budget within the upwind numerical floor for both grinds, shows the physical fast/slow two-grain exit structure (coarse slower than fine), and confirms the erratum by physical plausibility: every corrected intragranular diffusivity D_v=h_sl*d_s <= free aqueous diffusion (~2.2e-9 m^2/s) whereas the uncorrected reported values exceed it by orders.
column20: Model-vs-itself VERIFICATION only (mass/shape/plausibility); NOT a fit to experimental data and NOT the reported RMSE 5.81/6.23 -- reproducing that needs moroney2019's own Fig-6 exit-concentration digitization, which is not in the registry.

column18: Nondimensional exit concentration c_h vs time: model half-max time vs data t at c=0.5, and early-region RMSE.
column19: The leading-order asymptotic composite exit-concentration solution reproduces Fig 6's saturated plateau for t<1 and the wash-through timing (c=1/2 near the data midpoint) with early-region RMSE<0.15 — qualitatively.
column20: It does not capture the long-time diffusion tail (needs the outer solution, off-card) and is not a quantitative fit.

column18: Scalar closure outputs at fixed inputs: mu(363K), rho(363K), D per solute, K(Tref)/K80/K98 ratios, and h_sl at two flow rates.
column19: The pannusch2024 constitutive closures reproduce physical anchors: water viscosity ~3.15e-4 Pa.s and density ~962 kg/m^3 at 90C, Wilke-Chang D in 5e-9..1.2e-8 m^2/s, van't Hoff K(Tref)=Kref with weak T-dependence, and a positive Sherwood h_sl monotone in flow.
column20: It does not validate the extraction model's accuracy against any measurement (that is gate_pannusch_solver_mape) and does not test whether the closures reproduce real coffee behaviour.

column18: Per-solute MAPE (%) between simulated and measured fraction concentrations over 15 experiments x 6 time-fraction windows.
column19: Run with the authors' Table 2 per-solute fitted parameters, the two-grain multi-solute PDE solver reproduces the authors' own published fit MAPEs against the Schmieder kinetics (TDS 6.07, caffeine 4.59, trigonelline 7.85, CGA 4.98 %, each within ~3.5 pts).
column20: Does NOT demonstrate predictive accuracy on any independent/out-of-sample data — fit and evaluation are the same data.

column18: Bed mass-balance residual and yield monotonicity over 40 s, plus ordering of yield and strength at fixed beverage volume for Q=0.8/1.5/3.0e-6 m^3/s.
column19: The lumped flow-through bed solver (Eq 3.36) is mass-conserving (<2%) with monotone yield, and at a matched beverage volume a lower flow Q yields both higher extraction and higher strength than a higher Q.
column20: It does not reproduce or validate any measured espresso yield/strength magnitude; only conservation and the solver-guaranteed trend direction are checked, against no data.

column18: Mean percentage error (MPE, %) between the thesis's predicted and measured espresso yields per molecular-weight Deff class, digitized from Fig. 7.4 (~+/-0.5 pp).
column19: Across all 15 digitized Fig. 7.4 espresso conditions the thesis's reported medium-MW (non-fitted microstructural Deff) bed-scale prediction error is MPE <=14.3%, and the medium-MW single-Deff column has a lower mean MPE than the low- or high-MW columns.
column20: Does NOT reproduce the yields from any puckworks solver and does NOT test an independent lab/campaign — all data are from the same thesis and apparatus family.

column18: Deff class ordering (Table 4.9); K(20/50/80C) magnitude/monotonicity vs Table 4.10; t50 ordering by Deff and equilibrium-extent vs K in the stirred-vessel solver.
column19: Driven by the thesis's own parameter tables, the model's Deff spectrum orders low>med>high>vhigh (Table 4.9), the Arrhenius K(T) reproduces Table 4.10 to <0.05 and rises with T, and a higher-Deff species reaches 50% extraction faster with equilibrium rising in K.
column20: No out-of-sample or independent accuracy is demonstrated; the docstring's 'independent' label is an overclaim — it reproduces the source's own tables plus solver-guaranteed signs.

column18: Max abs difference between the numerical MOL sphere release (N=120) and the analytic Crank series at t=0.5..40 s.
column19: The spherical method-of-lines diffusion solver reproduces the Crank analytic fractional-release series (sphere into an infinite sink) to max abs error <1e-3 across seven times spanning the shot.
column20: It says nothing about whether the diffusion physics, Deff values, or partition BC match real espresso extraction — only that the PDE numerics are correct.

column18: Band membership of the 12 measured kappa values and the grind-ordering monotonicity across three densities.
column19: The thesis's measured tamped-bed Darcy permeabilities (Table 6.1) all sit in the 1e-14..1e-12 m^2 literature band (max >1e-13) and, at every initial bed density, a coarser grind gives higher kappa.
column20: It does not validate any permeability closure; passing implies nothing about predicting kappa, only that the measured data lands in a broad 2-decade band and orders with grind.

column18: Ordering of y0 vs the cameron and liang code-constant ceilings, and monotone decrease of the transcribed y0(grind) across six grinds.
column19: The thesis's extractable-inventory ceiling y0=31.7% sits above Cameron's per-bed EY ceiling (24.5%) which sits above Liang's single-immersion equilibrium ceiling (21.5%), and y0 decreases monotonically along the coarsening grind ladder (size-exclusion signal).
column20: It does not show the three ceilings measure the same physical quantity, nor validate a size-exclusion mechanism model; it confirms an ordering of source-reported numbers plus a monotone trend.

column18: Per-cell percent offset (mu_sobolik - mu_TR2001)/mu_TR2001 and (mu_khomyakov - mu_TR2001)/mu_TR2001 over the overlap, summarized by sign and median.
column19: sobolik2002 (Eq-5 dilute closure) joins khomyakov2020 as a third independent liquor-viscosity source; BOTH run systematically ABOVE the telisromero2001 Eq-10 closure across the Xw 76-90 %% / T 30-80 C overlap (sobolik median ~+54%%), so TR2001 is the probable LOW outlier (3-vs-1).
column20: Does NOT establish an absolute liquor viscosity nor validate any source at espresso TDS; records an inter-source ORDERING (TR2001 low) over the overlap only -- every source is soluble-coffee extract extrapolating toward water at espresso TDS.

column18: Per-cell percent offset (mu_khomyakov - mu_TR2001)/mu_TR2001 over the overlapping concentration/temperature cells, summarized by median/min/max and sign.
column19: Two independent measured coffee-extract viscosity datasets (khomyakov2020 kinematic converted to dynamic mu=rho*nu vs telisromero2001 Eq-10 closure) agree in sign/direction across their Xw 76-90% / T 295-365 K overlap but khomyakov runs systematically ~+37% (median 25-50%, max <60%) above TR2001.
column20: Does NOT establish which source is correct nor validate either at espresso TDS — only records a bounded, systematic inter-source magnitude discrepancy over the measured overlap box.

column18: Early-shot flow factor (mu_water/mu_espresso) and late/equilibrium flow factor under an algebraic Darcy Q~1/mu relation.
column19: Substituting the reference espresso viscosity (~1.3-2x pure water) into a Darcy Q~1/mu relation suppresses early-shot flow to ~0.5-0.8x the pure-water prediction while leaving the dilute late/equilibrium flow unchanged.
column20: It does not demonstrate that viscosity accounts for the observed early-shot flow bias, nor that the true early-shot mu ratio is 1.3-2x.

column18: Reference espresso dynamic-viscosity ratio to pure water at brew temperature, plus Newtonian-band membership.
column19: The transcribed reference espresso viscosity estimate (~4-6e-4 Pa.s, ~1.3-1.9x pure water 3.15e-4 Pa.s) exceeds pure water, stays within 1.0-2.5x, and lies in the Newtonian band (espresso 4-12% solids is below the ~36%-solids power-law onset).
column20: Does NOT measure or validate espresso-liquor viscosity — only that a transcribed, extrapolated reference value is above water and within a Newtonian bound.

column18: Spot-value comparison of the computed Eq (4)/(5)/(11) lines against the authors' own stated values.
column19: The sobolik2002 Eq (4)/(5) viscosity and Eq (11) conductivity COMPUTED lines reproduce the paper's own stated spot values (mu(omega=0.5,60C)~=0.019 Pa.s; mu(omega=0,20C)~=water; electrical conductivity peaks ~3.2 S/m near omega~0.4, 70 C) -- a transcription check of the digitized/computed closures of a concentrated soluble-coffee extract.
column20: Reproduces the SOURCE's own closure values only (soluble-coffee extract, omega = dry-coffee mass fraction); does NOT validate any espresso-liquor viscosity and promotes nothing.

column18: Density rho, specific heat cp, thermal conductivity k and thermal diffusivity alpha of soluble-coffee extract vs T and water fraction (plus a unit guard).
column19: The transcribed Telis-Romero 2000 linear thermophysical closures (rho/cp/k/alpha) reproduce the card's figure-endpoint / water-limit anchors within plotting accuracy, the typo-corrected Eq(6) alpha coefficient reproduces the Fig-5 endpoint while the printed one does not, and the fraction-vs-percent guard raises on percent input.
column20: It does NOT independently validate coffee-extract thermophysical properties (no external measurement) and says nothing about viscosity or flow — it confirms faithful transcription and flags the Eq-6 typo.

column18: Predicted eta at the Table-1 anchor cell and K at the Table-2 anchor cell (relative error vs the source's measured values), plus the bulk-shot mu/water ratio.
column19: The transcribed Telis-Romero 2001 Eq-10 (eta) and Eq-13 (K) closures reproduce the card's two quoted measured anchor points within the authors' stated fit error (eta<=3%, K<=10%), and the Eq-10 bulk-shot-TDS viscosity is ~1.06x pure water.
column20: It does not validate espresso-liquor rheology; it confirms faithful reproduction of the source's dilute-extract closure, not its applicability at espresso TDS.

column18: Newtonian dynamic viscosity eta (Pa.s) and power-law consistency index K (Pa.s^n) of soluble-coffee extract vs temperature and water fraction.
column19: The independently transcribed Telis-Romero 2001 Eq(10)/(13) viscosity closures reproduce the full digitized Table 1 (24 eta cells) and Table 2 (27 K cells) at about the authors' own reported fit error, cross-checking the digitization against the transcribed coefficients.
column20: It does NOT validate espresso-liquor viscosity nor independently confirm the closures against another lab/coffee — it only shows the two transcription paths of the SAME paper are mutually consistent.

column18: Darcy flow-suppression factor (mu_water / mu_local) implied by concentration-dependent liquor viscosity over a simulated shot's in-pore concentration field.
column19: Driving cameron2020's own in-pore liquor concentration field through the MEASURED Telis-Romero 2001 eta(c,T) grid, the in-pore liquor never approaches saturation (peak mu < 1.15x water), so the constant-water-viscosity assumption used by every registered flow model gives <8% outlet flow suppression and >0.95 depth-averaged flow factor at espresso conditions.
column20: It does NOT empirically demonstrate real espresso flow is insensitive to liquor viscosity nor validate cameron2020's concentration field — only that IF that field is right, the measured mu(c,T) correction is small.

column18: Per-shot post-bloom apparent-resistance decline ratio (peak/end of R=P/Q) vs the telisromero mu(TDS)/mu(water) ratio at bulk and early first-drip TDS.
column19: Across the 11 gagne2021 DE1 .shot traces the post-bloom apparent resistance R=P/Q declines by a robust median ~2.7x (every shot declines); the telisromero2001 viscosity ratio brackets this (~2.7x at Gagne's reconstructed ~15% early first-drip TDS, ~1.6x at bulk ~7.5% TDS), so a mu(TDS) viscosity-decline is quantitatively ADMISSIBLE.
column20: Does NOT establish viscosity as THE cause: the same magnitude is reachable by bed compaction/swelling, and with no independent TDS(t) the traces cannot separate the mechanisms (DEGENERATE). Gagne's own EY(t) reconstruction is endpoint-anchored/circular. The local mu ratio here is not the G10 shot-integrated Darcy error.

column18: Parameter bounds and monotonicity of the cubic K_r(S)=((S-S_r)/(1-S_r))^3 curve.
column19: The glass-bead analog closure loads with physically sane parameters (0<S_r<0.2, cubic n=3, alpha>0, K0>0) and yields a monotone-increasing relative permeability K_r(S) on [S_r,1].
column20: It does not validate any coffee retention/permeability curve and does not close G1.

column18: Pump/basket pressure drop dP (bar) vs flow Q over the working range, bounded against manufacturer free-flow debit and deadhead endpoints.
column19: The operative measured single-rig Waszkiewicz brewer quadratic (dP=aQ^2+bQ+c) stays inside the Ulka manufacturer envelope over the working flow band (dP below the 15-bar deadhead, free-flow > 1.90 g/s) — a bounds consistency check between two on-disk datasets.
column20: It does NOT establish the quadratic is the correct pump physics or holds outside the measured band (the true vibe-pump curve is concave), nor provide an independent DE1-specific characteristic.

column18: Polydispersivity S (moment ratio) vs grinder dial setting G.
column19: Recomputing S=<R><R^2>/<R^3> from Table 1's printed moment columns reproduces the paper's printed S values (max err <5e-3), and the printed S rises with dial G within each coffee.
column20: It does not establish that the moments/S are physically accurate or that the S(G) trend generalizes beyond these two coffees or this grinder.

column18: Mean grain radius <R> vs dial G (linear grind map).
column19: An OLS refit of <R>=beta*G+R0 to all 22 Table 1 samples is a high-R^2 (>0.99) monotone linear map whose predicted <R> at G=1 and G=11 bracket the measured range, reproducing the paper's corrected fit constants beta~5.805e-5, R0~1.380e-4.
column20: It does not demonstrate predictive accuracy on held-out settings, other grinders, or below G=1 (R0 is an extrapolation artifact, not a physical floor).

column18: Darcy-limit recovery of the Forchheimer momentum-equation root solver (code property).
column19: As k_I->infinity the Forchheimer quadratic solver returns the Darcy velocity q=|grad p|k/mu to rtol 1e-6, and a finite k_I (Zhou closure) yields q below the Darcy value.
column20: It says nothing about whether the Forchheimer closure or the k_I(k) law is correct for coffee.

column18: Peak Forchheimer number Fo_F = rho*k*q/(mu*k_I) evaluated along the recorded trace.
column19: Computing Fo_F along the real DE1 fixture-A tamped trace (Darcy k from the fitted kappa=1.196 via the kappa->SI chain, k_I from the ceramics-fitted Zhou closure) yields a peak Fo_F that is O(1), falls inside the backlog's 0.3-0.9 gusher-regime estimate, and exceeds the card's untamped espresso ceiling (0.0639) by >10x.
column20: It does not empirically validate that espresso flow is inertial nor measure Fo_F; only that the model's computed Fo_F on one uncontrolled community fixture is compatible with the backlog's higher estimate.

column18: Forchheimer number Fo_F band for the paper's worked espresso estimate.
column19: Recomputing the card's worked espresso estimate (<R> 145-276 um, phi_p 0.3-0.5 via percolation k, q 5.36-5.74e-4 m/s, Zhou k_I) reproduces the paper's reported Forchheimer-number band Fo_F 0.0161-0.0639.
column20: It does not measure Fo_F on any real shot nor confirm inertia actually matters in espresso.

column18: Pore-scale Reynolds-number band and apparent-k decline derived from mo2023 Fig 8a.
column19: Computing Re=rho(k grad p/mu)d43/mu from mo2023's digitized Fig 8a at their SPH conditions reproduces mo's reported Re band 0.84-3.86 and shows the apparent Darcy k declining with grad p (Forchheimer).
column20: It does not validate the Forchheimer closure against real espresso flow, and this Re is explicitly NOT interchangeable with wadsworth's Fo_F.

column18: Dimensionless collapse ratio k_measured / (k_star(phi_p,s_p)*phi_p^4.4) across the Table 1 grind rows, aggregated as a geometric mean.
column19: The paper's percolation permeability form k = k_star(phi_p,s_p)*phi_p^4.4 collapses their own Table 1 measured permeabilities to a geometric-mean ratio near 0.9 (gate window 0.7 < GM < 1.2).
column20: Does NOT independently validate k(R,phi) against out-of-sample or tamped-bed permeability data.

column18: Mass flow Q(t) at 9 bar vs time (long-run mean g/s and Pearson correlation, t>=15 s).
column19: With zero extra free parameters (static (P_c,Q_c) plus the dissolution sigmoid Phi(t)=m_d(t)/m0), Eq. 18 reproduces the measured 9-bar Q(t) ramp semi-quantitatively — long-run mean within 10% and correlation >0.90 over t>=15 s.
column20: A passing correlation does NOT show the mechanism is predictive on a new pressure/rig/coffee, nor that dissolution (rather than swelling, per the authors' own CT) drives the change.

column18: Steady-state basket-pressure vs mass-flow curve; recovered calibration parameters P_c, Q_c.
column19: Refitting the poroelastic equilibrium closure (Eq. 16) to the 11-pressure long-run pressure-flow curve recovers the calibration pair (P_c,Q_c) ~ (12 bar, 1.90 g/s) and reproduces the paper's ingested static calibration to <0.05 bar / <0.01 g/s.
column20: Does NOT independently validate the poroelastic model or transfer to another rig/coffee/grind — only confirms the refit reproduces the authors' own calibration on their own data.
